references.title: The Sum is Greater than the Parts: Ensembling Models of Student Knowledge in Educational Software

extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Models have generally been proposed in isolation.
extracted.Novelty of Model: Attempts to used Ensemble Logic to combine the predictions of different models and increase predictive performance.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Learners from assitments are treated as sequences of answers.
extracted.Models:
En-NeuralNet
En-RandomForest 
En-AdaBoost-J48 
En-Logit  
En-LinReg 
En-StepWiseReg
PFA
En-AdaBoost-DecisionStumps
BKT-EM
BKT-LessData
BKT-BF
En-Average BKT-PPS
CGS
Cslip
CFAR
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Student-level aggregation: {AUC: 0.7719 - En-NeuralNet}

Action-level aggregation: {AUC: 0.7693 - En-NeuralNet}
extracted.Comments:
Authors explain the multiple models that exist in KT and claim that ensemble approaches are relatively underexplored. For that reason, they test multiple alternatives using the Assistments Data.

The 5-fold CV results show NNs to outperform the remianing approaches whether the level of aggregation is at action level or at the student level.
extracted.Limitations:
Amount of data required for the models to be effective is unclear.

Needs validation from additional datasets.

Results obtained for this dataset are somewhat inconsistent with other results in the literature.

annotations.text: Most of the previous KT models used have shown inconsistent results. They argue that, in large part, these inconsistencies come from dataset size. Thus, authors explore how enemble methods could perform on a large dataset. They compare the performance of 8 student models and 8 ensembles and see that the best ensemble outperforms the single models by close to 10 percentage points.
